food_subgroup: Savoury cooked rice and lentil porridge with ghee

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Pongal. Nearest parent is a cooked rice-lentil porridge. A new concept covering South Indian pongal preparations is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Cooked grain and legume porridge, savoury style (closest parent)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of pongal is 1 bowl (approximately 250g) — a soft, mushy, creamy porridge of rice and moong dal flavoured with ghee, cracked pepper, and cumin, garnished with fried cashews and curry leaves.

nutrient_source.title: Pongal (savoury) — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F05100104; cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Exact match in HPB Foodo under food code F05100104 — Pongal (savoury/ven pongal), standard preparation.

gi_source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from cooked white rice and moong dal mixture; moong dal significantly lowers GI vs plain rice.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium approximately 470mg per bowl (250g) — moderate; pongal is a naturally low-sodium dish and the sodium comes from added salt which can be reduced.
hypertension.guidance: Suitable for hypertensive patients when prepared with reduced salt; avoid high-sodium sambar accompaniment for strict sodium restriction.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate-high GI (~72); glycaemic load per full bowl (250g) is approximately 40.5 — high; the moong dal provides fibre and protein which moderates the spike vs plain rice.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with diabetes should limit to a 150g portion and pair with a protein-rich side. The moong dal content provides protein and fibre which helps moderate the glycaemic response.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Ghee provides butyrate and fat-soluble vitamins; black pepper and ginger have anti-inflammatory properties; moong dal is a low-purine legume.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Pongal is excellent for patients with rheumatoid arthritis — ghee, ginger, and black pepper are traditional Ayurvedic anti-inflammatory ingredients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pongal (specifically Ven Pongal or Khara Pongal) is a beloved South Indian comfort dish from Tamil Nadu — a soft, creamy, savoury porridge made by pressure-cooking white rice and split yellow moong dal together until the mixture is completely amalgamated and mushy. It is generously flavoured with a hot ghee tempering of cumin seeds, cracked black pepper, fresh ginger, curry leaves, and golden-fried cashews. The dish is soft, warming, mildly spiced, and mildly nutty. In Singapore, pongal is a breakfast staple at South Indian restaurants and is served with sambar and coconut chutney.

confidence_reason: Exact match found in HPB Singapore Foodo Database under food code F05100104 — Pongal (savoury), standard preparation.
notes: Contains ghee (dairy/milk) and cashew (tree nut) — not vegan or dairy-free. Cholesterol approximately 26mg per bowl (250g) from ghee. Sweet sakkarai pongal (jaggery pongal) is a different dish with significantly higher sugar content — values here are for savoury ven pongal only.
